notes.note: Cumae, the old Greek colony on the bay of Naples, was the site of one of Cicero's seaside villas, much used as a writing retreat. In 46 BC he is there, within easy reach of Paetus, who lived nearby on the bay.
Marcus Caeparius, otherwise little known, is the common acquaintance who brings Cicero news of Paetus's health while crossing the Gallinarian Wood.
The Silva Gallinaria was a pine forest on the Campanian coast between Cumae and the mouth of the Volturnus, on the route between the bay-of-Naples estates.

Paetus is laid up with gout (Latin 'ex pedibus laborares'), a recurring affliction of the wealthy diner; Cicero turns it into a joke that at least the cook will not also be gouty, so a dinner is still possible.

notes.anchor: a guest at once least greedy and no friend to extravagant dinners
notes.note: A running theme of the Paetus correspondence of 46 BC: Cicero presents himself as a modest, frugal dinner-guest, partly as gallant reassurance to an ailing host and partly in keeping with the sumptuary restraint of the Caesarian moment.
